paragraphs.text: I want to bring attention to a few things: Some big credit card companies want to influence free speech by dictating what LEGAL objects, subscriptions and other online payment a consumer can or cannot purchase, with the excuse to avoid potential risks to their brand image and others, essentially preventing people from spending their money. This is concerning, since nowadays many things can be bought only online and this practice, gives the power to the major credit card companies to essentially censor products, platforms and people by cutting away their ability to receive payment from their customers. They should only follow the law dictated by the nations, not enforce their own rules, especially to just avoid supposed risks of brand image: if a purchase is legal, they don't have any responsibility over it so it cannot damage their brand image anyway. Credit card companies should only provide the tool to spend money online while following the law, nothing else. I also want to voice my concerns regarding the new emerging online safety acts, which are supposed to protect child but have very negative effects. These come with a great risk of being abused to repress the democratic process of free opinion building and free speech because of their broad definition of what is and isn't problematic and illegal. With these broad definitions, they can be abused to silence anybody that opposes ruling parties, uses strong language or has a different opinion, by categorizing their opinions and words as being problematic. Moreover, the tools used to verify the age of the users, if implemented, should absolutely not be operated by third parties, but by the government. This is because, if a third party is allowed to provide this service, it could sell this data to other companies, breaching the privacy of all EU citizens. Lastly I want to address the biggest problem with online purchases today: the volatility of the products. There should be laws that prevent third parties from revoking access from products that were bought by a customer or from destroying the product itself, with no reason. This completely goes against what a purchase is and it is a practice that has been gaining traction in recent years, for example in the video game industry (stop killing games, an initiative that has reached over 1.4 Million signatures, is a result of this problem) Video games are essentially a collection of data that is sold to the customer so that he can install it and use it on their digital device. Providing this data with CDs or digital downloads doesn't have any difference whatsoever, so buying video games online should be exactly like buying them in a store. But today when you buy a game online you technically buy only a license to play the game, which can be revoked at any point for any reason, meaning the customer doesn't own the game that it just bought, like he would have if he bought it in a store. The same principle should apply to all products sold online. Products that are sold online should follow the same rules of selling products in real life, with the necessary adjustments. For example on July 19 2023, the Android port of the game "The World Ends with You -Solo Remix-" was delisted and the IOS version on July 27, 2023. The game is no longer available on any storefront and I cannot install it on my new phone, even thought i bought it, while i cannot play it on my old phone because it broke. In summary, I think the DFA should include the following protections for consumers: The right for people to buy any legal products they want from an online store and for online store to sell any legal product they want. The protection of free speech on the internet. Parents, should be responsible for their children's access to the internet and its content. An online purchase shouldn't be allowed to be revoked or destroyed without a very good reason. Products like video games should be considered products that you buy and own, not a license.
